content.problemStatement:
Advanced Plotting Techniques will cover Multiple plots in further detail along with fancy plot types such as Boxplots and Heatmaps.

In this module - we will also introduce time series plots and 3D plots. However, we will keep our examples and content beginner friendly so that you get working knowledge of these techniques as a beginner.

This code creates a 3D scatter plot representing customer segmentation. Here's a breakdown of what it does and its marketing relevance:
- We generate sample data for 100 customers with three attributes:
   - Age: randomly distributed between 20 and 70
   - Income: randomly distributed between $20,000 and $100,000
   - Purchase Frequency: randomly distributed between 1 and 20 purchases per year
- Business Relevance:
   - Customer Visualization: This plot provides a quick visual representation of your customer base across three important dimensions.
   - Identifying Patterns: Even with this simple plot, you can start to see patterns or clusters in your customer data.
   - Outlier Detection: Unusual customers (e.g., young customers with high income and high purchase frequency) will stand out in this 3D space.

Again - do not worry about the specific code - we will learn some of these basics in the current module.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
